reason:
Both answers reach the same correct verdict (talk them out), recommend append-only ledger + CRUD instead of full event sourcing, flag the same domain pitfalls (proration, versioning, replay/consistency debugging pain), and suggest buying (Stripe Billing/Lago/Metronome) before building. Substantively they are very close.

The differentiator is presentation quality. Answer A is written in a broken, telegraphic pidgin style ("Team of 4 build billing, event sourcing wrong tool," "Money need audit trail," "This part legit") which is distracting and reads oddly for a busy professional audience — content is right but delivery undermines clarity and credibility. Answer B delivers the same substance in clean, well-organized, standard prose with clear headers and a crisper actionable checklist, making it easier to scan and act on. Neither answer explicitly calls out "enthusiasm for the pattern vs. the problem" as its own red flag, though both implicitly gesture at it (A via "learn on billing" rhetorical question, B via the closing "if the team wants to learn it" caveat) — a wash.

Given equal correctness and coverage but meaningfully better clarity/professionalism in B, B is the stronger answer.
